meta.title: bun
meta.subject: Bun — an all-in-one JavaScript runtime & toolkit

scenes.title: bun
scenes.footnote: Zig · Rust · C++   ·   built on JavaScriptCore

beats.narration: This is bun -- an all-in-one JavaScript runtime and toolkit.
One binary that is your runtime, your bundler, your transpiler, your test runner, and your package manager -- engineered, end to end, for speed.
It is built in Zig, Rust, and C-plus-plus, on top of JavaScriptCore -- WebKit's engine. We traced it through the source tree.
A typical JavaScript project assembles a toolchain -- Node for the runtime, esbuild or webpack to bundle, Babel or t-s-c to transpile, Jest to test, npm to install.
bun replaces all of it with one binary. The very same executable is the runtime that executes your code...
...the transpiler and the bundler -- TypeScript and JSX run with no build step at all...
...a test runner, and a package manager. bun install is, famously, many times faster than npm. One tool, one install, and no glue between them.

scenes.heading: An engine, and a core around it

beats.narration: At the centre is the runtime. bun does not ship its own JavaScript engine -- it embeds JavaScriptCore, the same engine that powers Safari.
Around it, a core written in Zig and Rust -- the part that makes bun bun. It owns process startup, module resolution, and the bridge into the engine.
Asynchronous work runs on a fast event loop, built on u-Web-Sockets rather than libuv -- one reason bun's H-T-T-P server benchmarks the way it does.
And bun implements the APIs you expect -- hundreds of Node-compatible modules, plus the web standards: fetch, WebSocket, streams. Most existing code simply runs.

scenes.heading: Native APIs, nothing to install

beats.narration: bun is not only fast at running other people's code -- it ships its own. A standard library of native APIs, with nothing at all to install.
A SQLite driver, built into the runtime, written in native code. Import bun-colon-sqlite, open a database, and query it -- no package required.
An H-T-T-P server -- Bun dot serve -- that speaks the Web standard Request and Response types, directly on that fast event loop.
And primitives like Bun dot file -- a lazy, zero-copy handle to a file on disk. The batteries are included, and every one of them is written in Zig or Rust.
What happens when you type bun run server dot t-s? Start the clock.
bun resolves the import graph -- every dependency the entry point reaches -- using its own fast module resolver.
Each TypeScript or JSX file goes to bun's transpiler -- which is not a separate tool, but the same engine the bundler uses.
It strips the types and lowers JSX, emitting plain JavaScript. No t-s-c, no Babel, no build directory -- it happens in memory, in microseconds.
That JavaScript is handed to JavaScriptCore to evaluate.
The engine runs the module -- and calls back into bun's native code for every Bun and Node A-P-I the program touches.
Your server is listening. From keystroke to running process, with no build step anywhere in between.

scenes.heading: bun, in four ideas
points: One binary — runtime, bundler, transpiler, test runner, package manager.
JavaScriptCore for execution; a Zig and Rust core around it.
A fast event loop, and native APIs with nothing to install.
TypeScript and JSX run directly — there is no build step.

beats.narration: So, bun in four ideas. One binary -- runtime, bundler, transpiler, test runner, and package manager.
JavaScriptCore does the executing; a core in Zig and Rust wraps it.
A fast event loop, and a standard library of native APIs with nothing to install.
And TypeScript and JSX that run directly -- no build step at all. One tool, engineered for speed from top to bottom.
